L00-01: On Mac you open Terminal from Spotlight, on Windows you install Windows Terminal or use WSL, and on Linux a terminal emulator is usually already installed — all three give you a command-line prompt where you type instructions directly to your computer.
L01-01: A file is a named collection of data stored on disk, exactly like a document in a filing cabinet — it can hold text, images, code, or any other information your computer can read.
L01-02: A folder (also called a directory) is a container that holds files and other folders, letting you organize your work the same way physical folders organize paper.
L01-03: A file path is the full address of a file on your computer, written as a chain of folder names separated by slashes, for example /home/alice/projects/notes.txt on Mac and Linux.
L01-04: A file type tells the computer what kind of data a file contains and how to open it, usually signaled by the extension at the end of the filename, such as .txt for text or .py for Python code.
L01-05: A program is a file containing instructions that the computer can execute — when you run a program, the computer reads those instructions and carries them out one by one.
L01-06: A terminal is a text-based window where you type commands and the computer prints responses, giving you direct control over your system without clicking through graphical menus.
L02-01: You find out where you are in the file system by running the pwd command, which stands for print working directory and prints the full path of the folder you are currently inside.
L02-02: You see what is in the current folder by running the ls command, which lists all the files and subfolders present there.
L02-03: You move into a folder with the cd command followed by the folder name, for example cd projects takes you inside a folder called projects.
L02-04: You go up one level to the parent folder by running cd .. — the two dots are a shorthand that always means the folder containing your current location.
L02-05: You return to your home directory from anywhere by running cd with no arguments or cd ~ — the tilde character is a shortcut that always refers to your personal home folder.
L02-06: You create a new folder with the mkdir command followed by the name you want, for example mkdir my-project makes a new empty directory called my-project.
L02-07: You create a new empty file with the touch command followed by a filename, for example touch notes.txt creates a blank file called notes.txt in your current folder.
L02-08: You delete a file with the rm command followed by the filename, for example rm notes.txt permanently removes that file — there is no trash can, so be careful.
L02-09: You delete an entire folder and everything inside it with rm -r followed by the folder name, where the -r flag tells rm to remove recursively through all contents.
L02-10: You copy a file with the cp command, writing the source filename first and the destination filename second, for example cp report.txt report-backup.txt.
L02-11: You move or rename a file with the mv command — mv old.txt new.txt renames it in place, and mv file.txt other-folder/ moves it into a different directory.
L02-12: Real workflows chain mkdir, cd, touch, mv, cp, and rm together in sequence — practicing them in combination is how the commands become second nature.
L02-13: A cheat-sheet is a single-page reference listing the commands you have learned — pwd, ls, cd, mkdir, touch, rm, cp, and mv — with a short description of each so you can look them up instantly.
L03-01: You read the full contents of a file in the terminal with the cat command followed by the filename, which prints every line to the screen.
L03-02: You see just the first lines of a file with the head command, which by default prints the first ten lines — useful when a file is too long to read all at once.
L03-03: You see just the last lines of a file with the tail command, which by default prints the final ten lines — handy for checking the newest entries in a log file.
L03-04: You print text directly to the terminal with the echo command, for example echo Hello World displays the words Hello World on your screen.
L03-05: You redirect the output of a command into a file using the greater-than symbol >, for example echo Hello > greeting.txt creates a file containing that text.
L03-06: You append text to an existing file without overwriting it by using >> instead of >, so echo Line two >> notes.txt adds a new line at the end.
L03-07: You can copy a file by redirecting cat output to a new filename with cat original.txt > copy.txt, which reads every byte and writes it to the destination in one step.
L03-08: You search for a word or pattern inside a file with the grep command, for example grep error log.txt prints every line in log.txt that contains the word error.
L03-09: You search for a pattern across many files at once with grep -r followed by the pattern and a folder name, which scans every file in that directory tree.
L03-10: You chain commands together with the pipe character |, which takes the output of one command and feeds it directly as input to the next, for example ls | grep .txt filters only text files.
L03-11: You count lines, words, and characters in a file with the wc command — wc -l gives just the line count, which is useful for quickly measuring how big a file is.
L03-12: Combining cat, grep, wc, and pipes lets you answer questions about files without opening an editor — for example piping grep output into wc -l counts how many times a word appears.
L03-13: You have now covered every core file-reading and writing command — cat, head, tail, echo, >, >>, grep, pipes, and wc — the foundation you will build every future skill on.
L04-01: Version control lets you save snapshots of your code over time so you can review what changed, undo mistakes, and collaborate with others without overwriting each other's work.
L04-02: You turn a folder into a git repository by running git init inside it, which creates a hidden .git directory that git uses to track every change you save from that point on.
L04-03: You choose which changed files to include in your next snapshot by running git add followed by a filename, or git add . to stage every changed file at once.
L04-04: You save a permanent snapshot of your staged changes by running git commit -m followed by a short message in quotes that describes what you changed.
L04-05: The core git loop is edit a file, run git add to stage it, then run git commit -m with a message — repeating this cycle gives your project a clear, recoverable history.
L04-06: git log shows your full commit history in the terminal — each entry lists the commit hash, author, date, and message so you can scroll through every saved change in order.
L04-07: git diff compares two versions of your files and shows exactly which lines were added or removed, letting you see what changed before or after a commit.
L04-08: git restore (or git checkout in older Git) undoes changes to a file, reverting it to the last committed state so you can safely discard mistakes without losing your history.
L04-09: GitHub is a website that stores your Git repositories in the cloud, making it easy to back up your code, share it with others, and collaborate on projects.
L04-10: git push uploads your local commits to a remote repository on GitHub, sharing your changes so others can see or download them.
L04-11: git clone downloads a full copy of a remote GitHub repository to your machine, and git pull fetches and merges any new commits that have been added since you cloned it.
L04-12: A Git branch is an independent line of development — you create one with git checkout -b or git switch -c so you can experiment freely without touching the working main code.
L04-13: git merge combines the commits from your feature branch into another branch (usually main), bringing your finished idea into the main codebase.
L04-14: Real teams pull the latest changes with git pull each morning, create a branch for each task, commit small focused changes throughout the day, and open a pull request on GitHub to get code reviewed before merging.
L04-15: A good commit message starts with a short imperative summary (under 72 characters) that completes the sentence "This commit will …", clearly describing what changed and why.
L04-16: A full Git cycle means initializing a repo, making changes, staging them with git add, saving them with git commit, and syncing with GitHub using git push and git pull.
L04-17: You now know how to track changes with git commit, explore history with git log, collaborate on GitHub with git push and pull, and work safely with branches and merges.
L05-01: Code is a sequence of precise instructions written in a language a computer can execute — the machine follows those instructions line by line to perform tasks.
L05-02: Different programming languages exist because different problems need different tools — Python excels at data and scripting, JavaScript runs in browsers, C handles low-level hardware, and each trades off speed, readability, and purpose.
L05-03: When you open a website your browser downloads HTML for structure, CSS for styling, and JavaScript for interactivity, plus images, fonts, and other assets — often dozens of separate files in one page load.
L05-04: The client is the device making a request (like your browser), and the server is the remote machine that receives it, processes it, and sends back a response.
L05-05: HTTP (HyperText Transfer Protocol) is the rule set that governs how a client sends a request and a server sends a response, using methods like GET and POST over the internet.
L05-06: An API (Application Programming Interface) is a defined set of endpoints a service exposes so other programs can send requests and receive structured data in return, without knowing how the service works internally.
L05-07: JSON (JavaScript Object Notation) is a lightweight text format using key-value pairs and arrays that APIs use to send and receive data because it is easy for both humans and code to read.
L05-08: A database is an organized system for storing and retrieving data persistently — most applications use one to save user accounts, posts, orders, or any information that must survive beyond a single session.
L05-09: SQL (Structured Query Language) is the standard language for querying and modifying relational databases — you write statements like SELECT, INSERT, and UPDATE to read or change records in tables.
L05-10: The frontend is everything the user sees and interacts with in the browser, while the backend is the server-side code that handles logic, authentication, and database access.
L05-11: A tech stack is the combination of languages, frameworks, and services a product is built with — for example, a common web stack might pair a React frontend, a Node.js backend, and a PostgreSQL database.
L05-12: The cloud means running your code and storing your data on remote servers owned by providers like AWS, Google Cloud, or Azure instead of on physical hardware you manage yourself.
L05-13: Deployment is the process of taking your local code and making it run on a server accessible to the public, typically by pushing it to a platform that builds and hosts it automatically.
L05-14: DNS (Domain Name System) translates a human-readable domain like example.com into the IP address of the server that hosts it, acting as the internet's phone book every time you visit a URL.
L06-01: A URL (Uniform Resource Locator) is the full address of a resource on the web, made up of a protocol (https), a domain (api.example.com), a path (/users), and optional query parameters.
L06-02: curl is a command-line tool that sends HTTP requests to any URL and prints the response — running curl https://example.com fetches that page's content directly in your terminal.
L06-03: curl flags let you control a request precisely — use -X to set the method, -H to add a header, -d to attach a body, and -i to include the response headers in the output.
L06-04: An HTTP status code is a three-digit number the server returns to describe the outcome of a request — 200 means OK, 404 means not found, and 500 means a server error occurred.
L06-05: Request headers are key-value pairs sent with every HTTP request that carry metadata like Authorization: Bearer your-token or Accept: application/json to identify who you are and what format you expect.
L06-06: An HTTP POST request sends data to a server to create or submit something — with curl you add -X POST and -d '{"key":"value"}' to include a body instead of just fetching a resource.
L06-07: You can hit a live public API with curl — for example, curl https://api.open-meteo.com/v1/forecast returns real JSON weather data you can inspect right in your terminal.
L06-08: An API key is a secret token you include in requests — usually in an Authorization header or a query parameter — that proves to the API you have permission to use it.
L06-09: GitHub exposes a REST API at api.github.com where you can authenticate with a personal access token in the Authorization header and retrieve data like your repositories or profile in JSON.
L06-10: API documentation lists each available endpoint, the required headers and parameters, and example requests — you translate those examples directly into curl commands to test them yourself.
L06-11: A 401 Unauthorized response means the server rejected your credentials — check that your API key is present, correctly spelled, and placed in the right header or parameter as the docs specify.
L06-12: Once you understand how to build a request with curl, rewriting it in code is straightforward — languages like Python (requests library) and JavaScript (fetch) use the same concepts of URL, method, headers, and body.
L07-01: Node.js is a runtime that lets you run JavaScript on your computer outside a browser — installing it gives you the node and npm commands you need to build real programs.
L07-02: The node REPL (Read-Eval-Print Loop) is an interactive terminal where you type JavaScript and see results instantly, line by line, without writing a file.
L07-03: You run a JavaScript file by typing node followed by the filename in your terminal, which executes every line of code in that file top to bottom.
L07-04: npm (Node Package Manager) is the tool that comes with Node.js for installing, managing, and sharing reusable code packages from the world's largest open-source registry.
L07-05: Running npm init in your project folder creates a package.json file that records your project's name, version, and dependencies — the foundation every Node.js project needs.
L07-06: You add someone else's code to your project by running npm install followed by a package name, which downloads it into node_modules and saves it as a dependency in package.json.
L07-07: node_modules holds all downloaded packages and should never be committed to Git — adding node_modules to a .gitignore file tells Git to ignore that folder entirely.
L07-08: Node.js has a built-in http module that lets you write a web server in plain JavaScript — you create a server, tell it how to handle requests, and listen on a port.
L07-09: You start your Node.js server by running node with your server file in the terminal; it stays running and handles incoming requests until you stop it.
L07-10: localhost is the hostname that points to your own computer, so when your server listens on a port like 3000, you visit localhost:3000 in a browser to see it.
L07-11: Routes let your server respond differently depending on the URL path requested — you check the path of each incoming request and send back the matching response.
L07-12: Your server can respond with JSON by setting the Content-Type header to application/json and writing a JSON string as the response body, which is how most APIs work.
L07-13: Your server can respond with an HTML string and set Content-Type to text/html, so the browser renders it as a real page instead of displaying raw text.
L07-14: Pressing Ctrl-C in the terminal stops your running Node.js server; you restart it by running the node command again, which is the standard edit-stop-restart cycle.
L07-15: Put your Node.js skills together by building a small server from scratch that handles multiple routes, serves JSON and HTML, and runs cleanly from the command line.
L08-01: Claude Code is Anthropic's command-line tool that lets you have a conversation with Claude directly in your terminal, where it can read, write, and edit your project files and run commands on your behalf.
L08-02: You install Claude Code by running npm install -g @anthropic-ai/claude-code in your terminal, which makes the claude command available anywhere on your computer.
L08-03: You start Claude Code by typing claude in your terminal inside your project folder, then type a message and press Enter to begin a back-and-forth conversation about your code.
L08-04: You can ask Claude Code to read and explain any file in your project — it opens the file, understands the code, and answers questions about what it does and how it works.
L08-05: Claude Code can create new files for you from a plain-English description — you describe what you want, and it writes the file with the correct name, location, and content.
L08-06: You describe a change you want in plain English, and Claude Code finds the right file, makes the edit, and shows you exactly what it changed before saving.
L08-07: You can describe a full feature or small app in plain language and Claude Code will create the necessary files, write the code, and wire everything together for you.
L08-08: A good prompt gives Claude Code specific context — what file to touch, what the current behavior is, and what you want instead — so it makes the right change on the first try.
L08-09: Breaking a big build into a sequence of small, focused asks — one task per message — keeps Claude Code accurate and makes it easy to catch mistakes before they compound.
L08-10: When Claude Code produces wrong output, you correct it by replying with what went wrong and what you actually want — treat it as a conversation, not a one-shot command.
L08-11: Always read the code Claude Code writes before accepting it: check that the logic is correct, nothing was deleted by accident, and the change matches what you asked for.
L08-12: When something breaks, paste the error message into Claude Code and ask it to fix the problem — it reads the relevant files, finds the cause, and proposes a targeted fix.
L08-13: Claude Code works alongside Git — you use git as normal to commit, branch, and review diffs, so every AI-made change is tracked and reversible just like your own edits.
L08-14: Claude Code is less useful for tasks that need live system access, precise pixel-level design, or decisions that require your personal judgment about product direction.
L08-15: Claude Code works best when you give it one clear task at a time, keep your files open so it has context, and treat its output as a draft you always review before moving on.
L08-16: Put everything together by using Claude Code to build a small project from scratch — creating files, editing code, fixing a bug, and reviewing every change along the way.
L09-01: Claude Skills are reusable folders containing a SKILL.md file that give Claude a set of step-by-step instructions for a specific workflow, so you can teach it your exact way of working.
L09-02: You create your first skill by making a folder with a SKILL.md file that describes a workflow you repeat often — Claude reads it and follows those steps whenever you invoke the skill.
L09-03: A great skill is specific, step-by-step, and written in plain language — it tells Claude exactly what to do, in what order, and what to check, leaving no room for guesswork.
L09-04: You share a skill with your team by committing the skill folder to your shared repository, so everyone on the team can invoke the same consistent workflow with Claude.
L09-05: The best builders keep their skills updated as their workflow evolves, break complex tasks into small focused skills, and review Claude's output every time to catch drift early.
L10-01: MCP (Model Context Protocol) is an open standard that lets Claude connect to external tools, databases, and services — giving it real-time access to data and actions beyond your local files.
L10-02: You add your first MCP connection by configuring a server in Claude Code's settings, which tells Claude how to reach an external tool and what actions it can perform through it.
L10-03: You can group related MCP servers into named sets and share a single config file so every teammate connects to the same tools without any manual setup.
L10-04: As your project grows, keeping MCP servers scoped and connections lean ensures Claude stays responsive instead of slowing down under the weight of unused integrations.
L10-05: You walk through a real MCP connection end-to-end, linking Claude to an external tool and verifying it works inside an actual coding session.
L11-01: CLAUDE.md is a plain text file you place in your project that Claude Code reads automatically, giving it persistent context about your codebase, conventions, and instructions across every session.
L11-02: Claude Code has no memory between separate sessions by default, so CLAUDE.md and structured notes are how you preserve important context and pick up exactly where you left off.
L11-03: The context window is the maximum amount of text a model can read and reason about at one time; once you exceed it, earlier content gets dropped, so knowing its limits helps you work more effectively.
L11-04: Writing a clear spec or plan before coding and creating concise handoff notes lets Claude pick up a task mid-stream without needing to rediscover decisions you already made.
L11-05: On large projects you keep Claude effective by breaking work into focused tasks, trimming irrelevant context, and feeding it only the information it actually needs at each step.
L12-01: A subagent is a separate Claude instance that Claude Code spawns to handle one focused task in its own isolated context, letting multiple pieces of work run in parallel without interfering with each other.
L12-02: A git worktree lets you check out multiple branches into separate folders on disk at the same time, so parallel subagents can each work on their own branch without overwriting each other.
L12-03: Hooks are scripts that run automatically when specific Claude Code events fire, and permissions define exactly which file operations and commands Claude is allowed to execute.
L12-04: Headless mode lets you run Claude Code non-interactively from a script or CI pipeline using the -p flag, so automated workflows can invoke Claude without any human sitting at a terminal.
L12-05: Different Claude models trade off speed, intelligence, and cost; choosing the right model for each task and integrating Claude Code into your IDE keeps your workflow both affordable and productive.
L13-01: Reading unfamiliar code means starting at the entry point, tracing the data flow, and asking what problem each piece solves rather than trying to memorize every line at once.
L13-02: To understand a function, read its name and parameters first to guess its purpose, then trace what it returns and how it changes state before diving into the implementation details.
L13-03: The debugging mindset means treating every bug as a hypothesis to test: reproduce the problem reliably, isolate the cause systematically, and verify the fix rather than guessing at random changes.
L13-04: console.log debugging is the simplest way to inspect what a program is actually doing: you print variable values at key points to see exactly where the data stops matching your expectations.
L13-05: Getting unstuck fast means searching error messages verbatim, reading official docs before forum posts, and knowing how to ask a precise question that describes what you tried and what happened.
L13-06: Working with an external API means reading its documentation, authenticating your requests with a key, and handling the responses and errors it sends back.
L13-07: Environment variables let you store secrets like API keys in a .env file that stays off version control, so your credentials never end up published in your code repository.
L13-08: The five non-negotiables every working developer follows are version control, environment variables for secrets, a README, error handling, and automated tests before shipping.
L13-09: Deploying means pushing your code to a platform that runs it on a public server so anyone with the URL can use what you built.
L13-10: A custom domain replaces the default hostname your hosting provider assigns with a URL you own, making your project look professional and easy to remember.
L13-11: Professional teams ship through a pipeline: code is reviewed, automated tests run on every change, and passing builds deploy automatically so bugs get caught before users see them.
L13-12: From here you choose a focused direction, whether frontend, backend, AI, or another speciality, and deepen your skills through deliberate practice on real projects.
L14-01: HTTP is request-response only, so the server cannot push updates to the browser without being asked; WebSockets open a persistent two-way connection that lets the server send data the moment something changes, which is essential for real-time features like multiplayer games.
L14-02: Before writing any code you sketch the game rules, data structures, and message types so you have a clear blueprint that prevents costly rewrites later.
L14-03: Setting up the game means creating the project, installing a WebSocket library, and writing the server entry point that will accept player connections.
L14-04: Building the rules means encoding win conditions, turn validation, and board state into functions the server can call to decide what happens after every move.
L14-05: Pairing two players means writing the matchmaking logic that holds the first connection in a waiting room and links it to the second connection when it arrives.
L14-06: Building the board means creating the HTML and CSS grid that renders the game state visually and updates instantly whenever the server sends a new state.
L14-07: Wiring it up means connecting the frontend click events to WebSocket messages and mapping every incoming server event to the correct UI update.
L14-08: Debugging live code in a WebSocket app means inspecting the message stream in browser DevTools and adding server-side logs to trace exactly which events are sent and received.
L14-09: Making a project finished means adding edge-case handling, cleaning up the UI, and ensuring the game resets cleanly so every new match starts from a valid state.
L14-10: Launching your game means deploying both the server and the frontend to a public host and verifying that two real players on different devices can connect and play.
L15-01: Claude Agents are custom Claude instances you configure with a specific system prompt, tool set, and scope so each agent is an expert at one job rather than a generalist.
L16-01: You can control Claude Code from a phone by connecting to a remote session or using a mobile SSH client, letting you trigger tasks, review output, and keep work moving away from your desk.
L17-01: Opus 4.8 is Anthropic's most capable model for complex reasoning, and Workflows let you orchestrate many specialized agents in sequence or in parallel so large tasks complete faster with higher quality.
